questions.question: Explain the mathematical foundation of RSA security and why integer factorization is considered computationally hard.
keyPoints: RSA uses the product of two large primes n = p × q
Integer factorization has no known efficient algorithm for large numbers
The best known algorithms (GNFS) run in sub-exponential time
Security depends on the size of n - larger n means exponentially harder to factor

questions.question: What is a chosen ciphertext attack (CCA)? Differentiate between CCA1 and CCA2 with an example.
keyPoints: CCA allows attacker to decrypt ciphertexts of their choosing
CCA1 (non-adaptive): queries made before seeing target ciphertext
CCA2 (adaptive): queries can depend on target ciphertext and previous responses
PKCS#1 v1.5 is vulnerable to CCA1, OAEP provides CCA2 security

questions.question: Describe the man-in-the-middle attack on Diffie-Hellman key exchange and suggest a countermeasure.
keyPoints: Attacker intercepts public values g^a and g^b
Establishes separate keys with both parties
Each party believes they share a key with each other
Countermeasure: Use digital signatures or certificates to authenticate public keys

questions.question: Explain the GCD attack on RSA. Under what conditions does this attack succeed?
keyPoints: Attack works when RSA moduli share common prime factors
Compute gcd(n1, n2) to find common factor p
This breaks both cryptographic systems using those moduli
Prevention: Use independent random primes for each key

questions.question: What is Wiener's attack on RSA? Under what conditions is RSA vulnerable to this attack?
keyPoints: Wiener's attack recovers private exponent d using continued fractions
Vulnerable when d < n^0.25
Also vulnerable if d is small relative to n
Countermeasure: Ensure d is sufficiently large (at least n^0.25)

questions.question: Differentiate between side-channel attacks and mathematical attacks on public key cryptosystems.
keyPoints: Mathematical attacks exploit underlying mathematical problems (factorization, DLP)
Side-channel attacks exploit implementation characteristics
Side-channel: timing, power analysis, electromagnetic radiation
Mathematical: brute force, cryptanalysis of the algorithm itself

questions.question: Explain the padding oracle attack (Bleichenbacher's attack) on RSA with PKCS#1 v1.5 padding.
keyPoints: Exploits difference in error messages for valid vs invalid padding
Attacker gradually decrypts ciphertext by modifying it and checking responses
Each successful oracle query reveals information about the plaintext
Countermeasure: Use OAEP padding or implement constant-time responses

questions.question: Explain the timing attack on RSA. How does it work, and what are the countermeasures against such attacks?
keyPoints: Timing attack measures execution time of decryption operations
Exploits variable time operations (modular exponentiation, Montgomery multiplication)
By analyzing timing variations, attacker can deduce private key bits
Countermeasures: constant-time implementations, random delays, blinding

questions.question: Discuss various attacks on RSA encryption and explain how proper padding (OAEP) provides security against chosen ciphertext attacks.
keyPoints: Plain RSA (no padding) is vulnerable to many attacks
Small message attack: if m^e < n, m can be recovered by e-th root
Hastad's broadcast attack: same message to multiple recipients
OAEP provides semantic security (IND-CPA) and CCA2 security
OAEP uses random padding and hash functions for security

questions.question: Compare RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) in terms of their underlying mathematical problems, key sizes for equivalent security, and known attacks.
keyPoints: RSA: integer factorization, 2048-bit key for moderate security
Diffie-Hellman: discrete logarithm problem, similar key sizes to RSA
ECC: elliptic curve discrete logarithm, 256-bit for equivalent RSA-3072 security
ECC provides better efficiency with smaller keys
All face similar attack categories: mathematical, implementation, side-channel
